summary:
Dark Hunt Fey are the class of Unseelie Fey whose bodies, senses, movement, and inherited magic are stabilized by the act of pursuit. They are not simply fey hunters, mounted nobles, hound-keepers, archers, or predators that chase prey. A Dark Hunt Fey belongs here when quarry-law is part of its nature: a target must be named, a horn must sound, a road must open, a hound must take scent, a boundary must be crossed, or the creature’s full body of powers cannot awaken.

Wild-hunt fey are the anchor example. This class may include horned riders, moon-road scouts, green-eyed archers, black hounds, fey horses, quarry-markers, erlking-like pursuers, hunt heralds, and Unseelie lineages that exist as parts of a chase rather than as solitary woodland predators. Some Dark Hunt Fey are humanoid riders. Some are mounts or hounds. Some are disembodied horn-calls, shadowed trackers, or masked figures who appear only when someone has already become quarry.

A field crew usually recognizes Dark Hunt Fey by movement that makes no ordinary geographical sense: hoofprints across water, hound tracks ending at a wall, arrows sunk into doors before the pursuers arrive, green mist in a road-cut, a horn heard from three directions at once, a rider passing behind a tree too narrow to hide it, or animals panicking toward the very path they should avoid.
tax:
Dark Hunt Fey sit directly beneath Unseelie because quarry-law is one of the phylum’s major dark fey persistence mechanisms. Where Blood Fey are stabilized by blood-price, Frightful Fey by cultivated dread, Spiteful Fey by provocation, Winter Fey by cold scarcity, and Gloaming Court Fey by witnessed obligation, Dark Hunt Fey are stabilized by pursuit under rule. The hunt is not their occupation. It is their ecology.

The class is separated from ordinary predators by legality. A wolf follows scent because it is hungry. A ranger tracks because it is trained. A Dark Hunt Fey follows because something has been named as quarry, because a road remembers the chase, because a horn has released the pack, because a court has declared the pursuit, or because the quarry violated a boundary that gives the hunt jurisdiction. Even when the hunt seems savage, its supernatural force usually depends on terms.

Dark Hunt Fey are separated from Frightful Fey because fear is secondary. Hound-cries and hoofbeats terrify prey, but terror is not usually the food. They are separated from Spiteful Fey because provoking pursuit is not the same as being metaphysically constituted by pursuit. They are separated from Gloaming Court Fey because a courtier may command a hunt, judge a quarry, or negotiate its release without belonging to the hunt-body itself. They are separated from Wild Fey because the Dark Hunt is organized by named quarry and repeated fey law, not by local instinct or free-ranging wilderness hazard.

A descendant belongs here only if hunt-law is inherited or metaphysically constitutive. “Uses a bow,” “rides a horse,” “owns hounds,” “serves as a tracker,” or “likes hunting mortals” is insufficient.
ap:
Dark Hunt Fey often appear as a moving company rather than a single silhouette. Even solitary specimens carry the shape of the missing pack around them: a rider with no visible mount but hoofbeats underfoot, a hound whose shadow has antlers, an archer whose arrows sound like horns, a scout trailed by green mist, or a pale horse whose saddle remembers a rider not currently present.

Humanoid members are often tall, lean, long-eared, and green- or moon-eyed, with antlered helms, black masks, pale hair, bark-dark limbs, or cloaks that stream backward like hunting banners. Their faces may be beautiful, expressionless, animal-alert, or hidden under lacquer, bone, leaves, or moonlit iron. Hands are commonly long and steady, built for reins, bows, spears, horns, or the ceremonial gesture of naming quarry.

Hound-like and mount-like members are not ordinary animals. Hunt hounds may have eyes like witchfire, paws that do not bend grass, ribs visible as green light, muzzles too long for their skulls, or voices that carry speech through baying. Hunt horses may be black, white, green-gray, bone-pale, or mist-bodied, with hooves that strike sparks from mud, manes like storm cloud, and nostrils cold enough to fog moonlight.

Field signs include hoofprints where no hoof could land, hound breath on glass, green mist in still air, arrows that do not match any local fletching, antler scratches above human reach, trampled grass that springs back after sunrise, and road dust found indoors around a sleeping quarry.
eco:
Dark Hunt Fey persist along paths that remember pursuit: old roads, deer runs, moonlit moors, gallows lanes, border forests, battlefield retreats, smuggler tracks, royal hunting preserves, ruined causeways, crossroads, mountain passes, underhill roads, and Feywild or First World margins where distance obeys story more readily than map.

They feed on ordinary food when embodied, especially meat, milk, roots, blood-warm drink, wild fruit, and offerings left at road shrines. Their deeper nourishment comes from pursuit itself: quarry-scent, oathbreakers fleeing judgment, trespassers crossing forbidden borders, fugitives taking the wrong road, worthy prey resisting well, and the clean completion of a chase under declared terms. The kill may matter, but the chase often matters more. Some hunts avoid killing at first, treating pursuit as contest, test, recruitment, or sport. Others ride as assassins and erase the quarry so completely that ordinary resurrection becomes difficult or meaningless.

Reproduction and persistence follow hunt-logic. Some Dark Hunt Fey are born within established fey hunt lineages. Some manifest from old roads where pursuit and disappearance have repeated for generations. Some are called into being by horns, titles, or monarchs. Some are transformed quarry: mortals, fey, beasts, or heroes who failed to escape and were remade as hounds, riders, archers, scouts, or stranger specialized hunters. Pathfinder wild hunt lore explicitly includes quarry transformed into new members of the hunt through wild reincarnation, with type changed to fey and loyalty transferred to the hunt.

A local Dark Hunt population weakens when quarry-law is satisfied or broken cleanly. Safe thresholds, sanctuary roads, dawn boundaries, cold iron horseshoes, fulfilled counter-oaths, broken horns, named exemptions, restitution for trespass, or the public release of the quarry can deny the hunt its jurisdiction.
beh:
Dark Hunt Fey are disciplined once the quarry is named. Before that moment, they may be eerie but restrained: waiting at a crossroads, watching from a ridge, sounding a horn only once, sending a hound to test scent, or offering terms through a masked rider. After release, they become coordinated pursuit. Hounds press the quarry toward open ground. Scouts cut off false trails. Archers punish hesitation. Riders appear where the road should not have allowed them to arrive.

They prefer rules. A quarry may be given a head start, forbidden from leaving the road, allowed sanctuary until moonset, required to carry a token, or told that no hunter but the hounds may draw blood. The easier the prey, the more elaborate the rules may become; Pathfinder wild hunt lore notes that monarchs may impose restrictions to make a hunt more entertaining. These rules do not make the hunt merciful. They make it legal.

Not every Dark Hunt Fey wants death. Some pursue to test courage, settle insult, retrieve stolen goods, enforce a boundary, improve their own numbers, or collect a quarry promised by a court. Others hunt to kill and are meticulous about preventing return: hiding remains, carrying bodies across planar thresholds, or transforming the defeated quarry into something that belongs to the hunt.

Among other Unseelie, Dark Hunt Fey are enforcers and border instruments. Gloaming Court Fey may declare quarry. Winter Fey may give the roads. Frightful Fey may soften a quarry through nightmares. Spiteful Fey may provoke the first trespass. Blood Fey may wait where the chase ends. But the Dark Hunt’s own loyalty is to horn, road, quarry, and the lawful shape of pursuit.
traitsText:
Type: Fey.

Senses: Low-light vision. Scent is common in hound and tracker descendants. Darkvision, see invisibility, blindsense, lifesense-like quarry detection, or shared-sense links may appear in lower taxa, but should not be assumed across the whole class.

Languages: Sylvan is standard among intelligent members. Aklo, Common, Elven, Giant, Druidic-adjacent hunting cant, and court horn-codes may appear in lower taxa. Hound and mount descendants may understand Sylvan or hunt commands without speaking.

Proficiencies: As fey: simple weapons and any weapons named in the descendant entry. Dark Hunt Fey commonly favor longbows, shortbows, spears, glaives, lances, knives, hunting horns, nets, reins, and natural attacks such as bite, gore, hoof, claw, or trample. Armor proficiency applies only when the creature is described as wearing armor.

Defensive Traits: Common descendant defenses include DR/cold iron, spell resistance, regeneration or fast healing while linked to a hunt, immunity or resistance to fatigue, bonuses on initiative and Perception while hunting, bonuses against illusion through shared senses, and planar acclimation. Pathfinder wild hunt creatures are strongly defined by cooperative hunt abilities such as wild hunt link and group pursuit. These should be assigned only where the lower taxon supports them.

Weaknesses: Common weakness patterns include cold iron, broken horns, lost quarry, dawn boundaries, sanctuary law, running water, fulfilled counter-oaths, iron horseshoes, severed hunt links, denied line of pursuit, or a declared hunt-rule turned against the hunter. A Dark Hunt Fey without legal quarry should often be weaker, more ceremonial, or unable to use its strongest powers.

Skills: Perception, Survival, Stealth, Ride, Acrobatics, Intimidate, Knowledge, Sense Motive, Handle Animal, and Fly are common skill directions. Descendant taxa often receive racial bonuses to Survival when tracking a named quarry, Perception while in a hunt link, Stealth on moonlit roads, or Ride and Fly during pursuit.

Environment: Any land, especially forests, moors, mountains, roads, ruins, borderlands, old hunting preserves, crossroads, gallows lanes, underhill roads, and First World/Feywild borderlands.

Organization: Solitary, pair, scout, hound-pair, riding band, archer line, hunt, retinue, monarch’s company, or full dark hunt.
abilities:
Quarry-Naming (Su). A Dark Hunt Fey’s strongest powers often require a creature to become quarry. Lower taxa should define the trigger: trespass, broken oath, accepted challenge, stolen object, court command, horn-call, marked footprint, named target, or voluntary entry into a hunt-road. Once named, the quarry may become easier to track, harder to hide, vulnerable to hunt-specific abilities, or subject to special movement rules. A creature should have a meaningful way to avoid, contest, satisfy, or be released from quarry status.

Wild Hunt Link (Su). Cooperative descendants may share senses, initiative benefits, perception, anti-illusion benefits, regeneration, or coordinated targeting while part of the same hunt. Pathfinder wild hunt creatures use wild hunt link as a defining group mechanic, with monarch versions extending the range and strengthening linked members. In this taxonomy, the link should be assigned to organized hunt taxa, not every lone hunter. Severing distance, breaking the horn, killing the leader, crossing sanctuary, or removing quarry status may interrupt it.

Hound-Cry Pressure (Su). Hound-like or horn-bound descendants may exhaust, frighten, disorient, or drive quarry by sound. Mechanically this may be a fear effect, fatigue effect, compulsion to move, penalty to Survival checks, or disruption of rest. The ability should specify range, save, duration, and whether it affects only named quarry. It should be weaker against creatures inside sanctuary, behind cold iron thresholds, or under a valid counter-oath.

Moon-Road Passage (Sp or Su). Many Dark Hunt Fey move along roads that are partly physical and partly fey: moonlit lanes, hoofbeat echoes, gallows paths, deer runs, border stones, fog roads, and underhill ways. This may be modeled as dimension door, tree stride, shadow walk, plane shift, or teleportation with strict route and quarry limitations. It cannot open anywhere at will; the passage needs road-memory, hunt-law, quarry-scent, or a horn-call.

Wild Gaze (Su). Some greater hunt descendants may carry a gaze that paralyzes, confuses, dazes, panics, or otherwise fixes victims into the logic of pursuit. Pathfinder wild hunt monarchs have a powerful wild gaze that affects different creature types differently and can exclude chosen visible creatures. Use this only for high-order hunt fey or major descendants. Lesser members should use narrower fear, mark, or tracking effects.

Horn of Release (Su). A horn, cry, whistle, antler-call, hoofbeat rhythm, or leader’s command may begin, redirect, suspend, or end a hunt. Lower taxa should define who may sound it, what action it requires, how far it carries, and what effect follows. Breaking, stealing, silencing, answering, or lawfully counter-sounding the horn should provide counterplay.

Road-Folding Pursuit (Su). While pursuing lawful quarry, Dark Hunt Fey may treat difficult ground, distance, and intervening paths as less restrictive than ordinary terrain. This may appear as increased speed, ignoring difficult terrain, tracking without penalty, closing distance over roads, or appearing at a plausible exit before the quarry arrives. It should not become unrestricted teleportation. Running water, iron-marked crossings, dawn, sanctuary, and no-road terrain should matter unless the descendant specifically overcomes them.

Archer’s Certain Shot (Ex or Su). Archer descendants may ignore some concealment, cover, or range penalties against named quarry, especially while linked to scouts or hounds. Pathfinder wild hunt lore includes archers as a distinct role within the hunt, and wild hunt groups are most dangerous when their members’ abilities combine. Lower taxa should define whether the ability works only through line of sight, shared senses, a hunt link, or a quarry mark.

Stag-Step and Hoof Thunder (Ex or Su). Rider, horse, or antlered descendants may move through forests, slopes, snow, scree, and underbrush without slowing, or may use charge, trample, gore, or mounted movement as signature attacks. This ability should stay anatomical: hooves, antlers, mounts, or rider-bond must support it. It is not appropriate for every scout or archer.

Capture Bargain (Su). Some Dark Hunt Fey offer terms before or during pursuit: run until dawn, remain on the road, surrender the stolen object, name the oathbreaker, give up a memory, take another’s place, or survive three horn-calls. If accepted, the bargain may bind both quarry and hunters. Breaking the bargain should have consequences for the side that breaks it, including loss of hunt authority.

Wild Reincarnation (Su). Greater hunt leaders may remake defeated quarry into new hunt fey. Pathfinder wild hunt monarchs can reincarnate slain creatures into wild hunt fey, changing the creature’s type to fey and binding it to the hunt unless powerful magic reverses the effect. In this catalogue, use wild reincarnation sparingly for monarch, erlking, or high-order taxa. It should define eligibility, save, timing, memory retention, and the method of reversal.

Sanctuary Denial (Su or Weakness). Dark Hunt Fey often cannot press their strongest powers across a valid sanctuary: a dawn-lit threshold, guest-right hall, named refuge stone, chapel road, cold-iron gate, or oath-protected bridge. Lower taxa may define sanctuary as a weakness, a required save, or a condition that suppresses quarry abilities. This weakness is essential because it makes field law and route knowledge matter.
bg:
Dark Hunt Fey matter because they turn travel into jurisdiction. A party being chased by wolves asks, “Can we outrun them?” A party hunted by Dark Hunt Fey must ask, “Who named us quarry, what rule did we break, where is sanctuary, and what road are we no longer allowed to trust?” The difference is often the difference between a chase scene and a taxological hazard.

Rangers, route-makers, shrine-keepers, Pall-Brokers, heralds, and caravan captains record Dark Hunt signs because the countermeasures are procedural as well as martial. Carry cold iron horseshoe nails. Learn which roads are sanctuary after dawn. Do not answer a horn from the dark. Do not accept “sporting terms” without witnesses. Do not steal from a hunt-road shrine. If hounds cry from three directions, find the rule before finding the trail.

This class also keeps the taxonomy clean. Wild-hunt fey, erlking riders, fey hounds, moon-road scouts, and quarry-bound archers are related by pursuit ecology, not by sharing one body shape. Their common inheritance is not hooves, bows, or antlers. It is the fey law that a named quarry makes the world rearrange itself into a chase.
